perturbation.original_text: 1.34 "Pro Rata Ownership" means for any Shareholder, the ratio equal to the total number of Shares held by such Shareholder at any given time to the total number of outstanding Shares at such time.
perturbation.changed_text: 1.34 "Pro Rata Ownership" means for any Shareholder, the ratio equal to the total number of Shares held by such Shareholder at the Effective Date to the total number of outstanding Shares at the Effective Date.
perturbation.explanation: This change introduces a contradiction in the definition of 'Pro Rata Ownership'. Originally, it was defined as the ratio at 'any given time', allowing for adjustments as shareholding changes. By changing it to the 'Effective Date', the Pro Rata Ownership becomes fixed, which creates an In-Text contradiction in sections of the agreement, such as Section 3.2, that depend on the flexibility of 'Pro Rata Ownership'.
perturbation.contradicted_text: All cash requirements of the Company shall be satisfied from cash generated by the operations of the Company, from external financing (on a non-recourse basis and without guarantees of the Shareholders or their Affiliates) procured by the Company in its own name, from financing by the Shareholders (the burden of such financing shall be allocated to a Shareholder based on Pro Rata Ownership) and from the initial capitalization.
perturbation.justification: The modified text changes the definition of 'Pro Rata Ownership' from a dynamic ratio calculated at 'any given time' to a fixed ratio based on the 'Effective Date'. This creates a contradiction with the contradicted text, which states that financing burdens are allocated to shareholders based on 'Pro Rata Ownership'. If the 'Pro Rata Ownership' is fixed at the 'Effective Date', it no longer accurately reflects the current shareholding ratio, creating an inconsistency in how financing burdens should be allocated throughout the term of the agreement. This constitutes an in-text contradiction as per the definition provided.

perturbation.original_text: 4.2 Composition of the Board. The Board shall consist of five (5) members (each, a "Director"), three (3) of whom shall be nominated by AFSL and two (2) of whom shall be nominated by TPH-A, and each Shareholder shall vote all of its Shares in favor of the election of the Directors nominated by the other.
perturbation.changed_text: 4.2 Composition of the Board. The Board shall consist of five (5) members (each, a “Director”), three (3) of whom shall be nominated by AFSL and two (2) of whom shall be nominated by TPH-A, and each Shareholder may vote its Shares in favor of the election of the Directors nominated by the other.
perturbation.explanation: By changing 'shall' to 'may' in Section 4.2, this modifies the obligation to vote for the other party's nominees. This introduces a contradiction with Section 4.8. This creates an In-Text contradiction since it affects the enforceability of the Board composition agreement, leading to uncertainty in the management of the company. Because 'may' is permissive, a shareholder could choose to vote against the other party's nominations, potentially disrupting the intended balance of power on the Board. This undermines the collaborative management structure envisioned by the joint venture and contradicts another section.
perturbation.contradicted_text: Each Shareholder shall take all actions necessary to cause the Directors nominated by it to abide by and implement all of the provisions of this Agreement.
perturbation.justification: The change from 'shall' to 'may' in Section 4.2 introduces a direct contradiction with the obligation outlined in the Contradicted Text. Originally, each shareholder was required to vote in favor of the other's nominees ('shall vote'). The modified text makes this permissive ('may vote'), creating uncertainty about whether the shareholders are obligated to support each other's nominees. This contradicts the Contradicted Text, which requires shareholders to take all actions necessary to ensure their nominated directors implement the agreement, including the board composition provisions. By allowing shareholders to potentially vote against nominees, the change undermines the intended balance of power and enforceability of the agreement, creating a direct conflict.

perturbation.original_text: 5.3 GaN Equipment. During the term of this Agreement, TPH-A/TPH shall be responsible for the costs and expenses agreed by the Parties to maintain and/or procure the equipment for wafer processing specified in the Appendix 2 attached hereto and as amended from time to time upon the mutual agreement of the Parties (the "GaN Equipment").
perturbation.changed_text: 5.3 GaN Equipment. During the term of this Agreement, AFSL/FSL shall be responsible for the costs and expenses agreed by the Parties to maintain and/or procure the equipment for wafer processing specified in the Appendix 2 attached hereto and as amended from time to time upon the mutual agreement of the Parties (the "GaN Equipment").
perturbation.explanation: By changing AFSL/FSL being responsible for the GaN equipment, instead of TPH-A/TPH, is contradicting the TPH's Right to Require the Purchase of the Shares from FSL or AFSL in Section 9.2. It creates an In-Text contradiction if AFSL/FSL do not procure or maintain the GaN Equipment.
perturbation.contradicted_text: As longs as TPH-A and TPH are not in material and continuing breach of this Agreement or the Ancillary Agreements, TPH-A or TPH may trigger the option to purchase the Call Shares (the "Call Option") by providing a written notice of the exercise of the Call Option (the "Call Exercise Notice") simultaneously to the Company, FSL and AFSL, which notice states (i) such Party's bona fide intention to exercise the Call Option, and (ii) the date on which such Party intends that the Call Closing Date occur.
perturbation.justification: The original text assigns responsibility for GaN equipment to TPH-A/TPH. The changed text shifts this responsibility to AFSL/FSL. This creates a contradiction because the contradicted text grants TPH-A/TPH the option to purchase shares from FSL/AFSL as long as TPH-A/TPH are not in breach of the agreement. If AFSL/FSL are now responsible for the GaN equipment and fail to maintain it, this could trigger a situation where TPH-A/TPH would be considered in breach if they didn't take over maintenance or procurement, thus impacting the call option. Thus, there is an Ambiguities - In Text Contradiction by changing who has responsibility and contradicting the TPH's Right to Require the Purchase of the Shares from FSL or AFSL in Section 9.2. It creates an In-Text contradiction if AFSL/FSL do not procure or maintain the GaN Equipment. This fits the definition of 'Ambiguities – In Text Contradiction: Ambiguities occur when key terms or obligations are defined inconsistently in different parts of a contract, making enforcement difficult or interpretation unclear.'
